questions.q: Compare the final charge from charging a neutral conductor by conduction versus by induction.
options: Both give the opposite sign to the charging object
Conduction gives the same sign (by contact); induction gives the opposite sign (no contact)
Both give the same sign as the charging object
Conduction gives the opposite sign; induction gives the same sign
questions.explanation: Conduction needs contact and leaves the conductor with the SAME sign as the charging object; induction works without contact (charges separate, the conductor is earthed, then the object removed) and leaves the OPPOSITE sign. This same/contact vs opposite/no-contact contrast is a classic discriminator.

questions.q: Two parallel plates 0.02 m apart have 400 V across them. Calculate the field strength, then the force on a 5×10⁻⁶ C charge placed between them.
questions.explanation: E = V/d = 400/0.02 = 20 000 V m⁻¹ (uniform between the plates). Then F = Eq = 20 000 × 5×10⁻⁶ = 0.1 N. The two-step 'field first, then force' is the standard parallel-plate method and the basis of particle acceleration in Module 6.

questions.q: Two point charges feel a certain force. Predict the new force if one charge is tripled and the distance is halved.
questions.explanation: By Coulomb's law F ∝ q₁q₂/r². Tripling one charge gives ×3; halving the distance gives ×(1/0.5²) = ×4. Combined: 3 × 4 = ×12. Forgetting to square the distance factor (using ×2 instead of ×4) is the trap.

questions.q: A charge of 120 C flows through a resistor in 40 s with 12 V across it. Calculate the current and the power dissipated.
questions.explanation: Current I = q/t = 120/40 = 3 A. Power P = VI = 12 × 3 = 36 W. (Resistance would be R = V/I = 4 Ω.) Keep current as charge per time and power as voltage times current — mixing the formulas is the common error.

questions.q: A 9 V supply connects a 2 Ω resistor in series with a parallel pair of 3 Ω and 6 Ω. Calculate the total resistance.
questions.explanation: First the parallel pair: 1/R = 1/3 + 1/6 = 2/6 + 1/6 = 3/6 = 1/2, so 2 Ω. In series with the 2 Ω resistor: R_T = 2 + 2 = 4 Ω. Reduce the parallel section first, then add the series part — treating all three as series (11 Ω) is the trap.

questions.q: State which conservation principle Kirchhoff's voltage law expresses.
questions.explanation: Kirchhoff's voltage law (ΣV = 0 around a loop) expresses conservation of energy — a charge gains as much energy from the source as it loses across the components round the loop. Kirchhoff's current law (ΣI = 0 at a junction) expresses conservation of charge.

questions.q: A long straight wire carries conventional current to your right. Using the right-hand grip rule, determine the field direction directly above the wire.
questions.explanation: Point the right thumb to the right (along the current); the fingers curl so that above the wire they point out of the page toward you (and into the page below it). The field forms complete circles around the wire; getting this direction right is essential for the motor effect in Module 6.

questions.q: Distinguish electric field strength from electric force.
options: Field strength E = F/q is a property of the location; force F = Eq is what a specific charge experiences
Force is a property of the location; field is what a charge feels
Field strength is measured in newtons; force in N C⁻¹
questions.explanation: The electric field strength E = F/q is the force per unit charge — a property of the location, existing whether or not a charge sits there. The electric force F = Eq is the actual push or pull on a specific charge placed in the field. The field describes the location; the force describes a particular charge in it.

questions.q: A solenoid of 300 turns and length 0.2 m carries 4 A (μ₀ = 4π×10⁻⁷). Calculate the magnetic field inside it.
questions.explanation: B = μ₀NI/L = (4π×10⁻⁷ × 300 × 4)/0.2 ≈ 1.51×10⁻³ / 0.2 ≈ 7.5×10⁻³ T. The field grows with current and turns and shrinks with length. Forgetting to divide by the length is the common error.

questions.q: An iron core is inserted into a current-carrying solenoid. Explain what happens to the magnetic field and why.
options: The field disappears because iron blocks magnetism
The field becomes much stronger, because the iron's domains align with the coil's field and add to it
The field is unchanged because the core is solid
questions.explanation: The iron is ferromagnetic: the solenoid's field aligns the iron's domains, which add their own magnetism, multiplying the overall field (often hundreds of times). This is how an electromagnet is made — combining the current's magnetic field with the magnetisation of the iron core.
